speaker: interviewee
text: I'm going to use the bathroom real quick.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I'll count it back.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's number 3.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling on four.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number 3 of the interview with Richard Pick in Mexico City, Mexico. You had a couple items you wanted to go back--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there's one--

speaker: interviewer
text: --to in Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: --item I forgot to mention. Hitler came to Stuttgart on his birthday, on his 50th birthday, which was April 20th, 1940. And since he went to the Rathaus in Stuttgart-- which is the, the, the town hall. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my grandmother's home-- house, was right on the Marktplatz, on the main square, where I stayed with my grandmother. All the, the houses on the Marktplatz had to be [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] fixed with flowers and bouquets and the, the swastika flag. Since we, of course, didn't have a swastika flag and we, we, [LAUGHS] we just didn't see any sense of going through these motions, we actually were contacted already a few days before by the Gestapo. Someone came to our home, to Marktplatz 19. Number 19 was the address. And they told us that Hitler was coming and that, that they would send us a couple of people to take care of the-- all the flower trimmings and the swastika flag, which was hung out of the house, because it was hung out of all the other houses, and that we, we could not look out of the window or leave the house after 8 o'clock in the morning. We should stay inside until in the afternoon. Hitler came actually around 12:00. And so we were not supposed to look out of the window, which we didn't. And there were two Gestapo officers who came to our house. One was on the roof, and the other one was inside with us. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The other little story I wanted to mention is before I left for Munich, we needed-- as the last [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] requirement, we needed the authorization by the Gestapo, by the German Secret Police. They looked over all our papers. And then they gave us also the authorization to leave. I knew that process through the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] institution where-- I worked for, the Judische Auswandererstelle. So I went to the Gestapo with all my papers, and-- including, of course, the, the visa for, for Spain, and the letter from the Reiseburo Rominger. And I went. And I saw Inspector Koch was his name. 'Coach' you would say in English, like the mayor's name of, of New York. He had the same name. And there, you went inside. You had to stand about 10 feet in front of the big desk, you know, and to come to attention. And you had to say, 'Jude, Jude Richard Israel Pick, [SPEAKING GERMAN].'

speaker: interviewer
text: And he looked at me. He looked over the papers. He'd say, where do you go to? I said, I go to Liberia. He said, Liberia, is it? Where is Liberia? And he had a huge world map hanging there, you know. And I showed him-- I pointed out to him, well, Liberia was in Africa. So he traced it back. He said, where do you leave from? I said, from, from, from Spain. I will leave from, from Cadiz, where the, the freighter is going to leave from. He said, you are going to go to Spain? Are you sure you're going to leave from Cadiz? I said, listen, Inspector, Herr Inspector, I-- this is the way it's planned. I mean, if I could get an earlier ship, maybe from Barcelona-- because I-- I'm going to-- I intend to fly to Barcelona, then I, I will take a ship from Barcelona. He said, from Barcelona? Then you will have to pass Gibraltar. I said, I don't know, Herr Inspector. He said, well, look here. I mean, you have to pass Gibraltar. I said, all right, Inspector. He said, well, what are you going to tell the English? I said, I don't know. Are there English there? He said, yes, there are English there. He said, what will you tell them? I said, I don't know, Herr Inspector. Listen, I mean, remember that at the time, I was 19 years old. He said, well, what are you going to tell them? Do you like it here?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, do you like it here? If I say yes, he can say, why don't you stay? If I say no, he'd say, why, why do you leave? I mean, it's-- you know, I mean, I was-- I was between a, a, a, a rock and a hard place. So I, I, I, I, I, I, I, I couldn't say anything anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was starting to, to smile. And he said, OK. And he gave me the stamp. And he-- I was so-- I was so excited, you know. I was so nervous. I said, auf Wiedersehen, Herr Inspector. And he said [SPEAKING GERMAN]. That auf Wiedersehen, you know, it means 'until we see each other again.'  It's a German saying for, for farewell, for goodbye, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he played with the words. And he said, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. I think we will never see each other again. So I said, [SPEAKING GERMAN], Herr Inspector. And I went outside.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And then we-- I went-- to get back again, to the-- to the plane ride. We-- that plane landed in, in Lyon, which was the, the Vichy government in, in France at the time. And I mean, every time we went down, you know, I mean, I thought, my goodness. What's going to happen now? And we stayed there for about, oh, I'd say maybe a half an hour or so.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we went on to Barcelona. And when the plane landed in Barcelona, I mean, it was the first time in my life I'd seen palm trees. And I was going outside. I was still sitting there in my-- in that heavy, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] plastic raincoat, you know. And it was hot. It was-- it was in July. I went outside. I got my two suitcases. I walked towards the, the airport-- little building. It was a little building there. And I was standing there. And the people were going on a-- on a bus. There was a bus standing there. And I was standing here with my two suitcases. I didn't know what to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So someone said to me, don't you want to go on the bus, too? I said, it cost money, you know. I forgot to mention that we only could leave Germany with 10 marks-- 10 German marks, which, at the time, officially were worth $2.50.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, I, I don't know what to do, you know. I said, I don't have money, I mean, to-- he said, that doesn't cost anything, you know? So, so when I heard it doesn't cost anything, anything went, you know. I went in the bus.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to Barcelona. And then in Barcelona, they dropped us off at the, the, the office of the-- of the Lufthansa, which was on the main street, the Rambla de Cataluna, in Barcelona. It's a beautiful street. And [DRINKS] I didn't know what to do. I went inside that Lufthansa office. And I talked to a girl there. I said, I just arrived here. And I said, I'm a refugee, and I'm Jewish. And, I mean, can you-- I'm waiting here to get by-- until the, the, the, the ship comes, you know, to take me to, to Liberia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she said, well, there isn't anything I can do for you. The manager is not here. But why don't you come back tomorrow morning when we open at 9 o'clock? So I said, thank you very much. I mean, they spoke German at least, you know, because everyone else spoke Spanish. So I went outside. It was in the afternoon. And at that time, you know, planes took much longer than nowadays. [LAUGHS] And I was sitting down there on a bench. And it was getting dark. And I figured, well, I might just as well bed down. So I slept on the bench.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had one suitcase I, I put on the bench. And I put my, my head on it, you know. And the other one, I had down. And I, I, I had my hand on it. And I slept there-- it was warm. I slept there the night on the Rambla de Cataluna. And the next morning, of course, when it [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] got light, I, I got up, and I waited for the office to open. At 9 o'clock, I, I went inside. And there was the manager. And to make a long story short, he said, all right. He said, I give you a job. You will not make very much. But you could carry luggage at the airport. So he-- they, they paid me a little money. I went to the airport every day, and I carried luggage.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then my friend, that Herbert Dreyfuss, he told me, when-- once you land in Spain, send me a telegram what I could bring, you know. So I asked them in Spain, I mean, what-- you know, what you can sell here, convert into money? And I couldn't believe, they'd say-- I mean, I could only bring something you could get in Germany, you know? So they told me fountain pens, which I told back, that's out. And they told me, horseradish. Horseradish? Just like the way you hear it, horseradish, the, the plants, the roots-- Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes? So I sent him a telegram-- to Herbert. From Spain, there was no problem for me to send a telegram. I sent it from the Lufthansa office. They sent telegrams there all day long, you know. So I sent him a telegram.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, landed safely. Bring as much horseradish as you can. He thought I, I must have gone wacky, you know? [LAUGHS] So a few days later, maybe three or four days later, or five days later, he arrived. And he checked in there with the Lufthansa. And I told them at the Lufthansa when he come, they should tell me-- my friend. And then we were two. And we-- he, he also carried luggage. The two of us carried luggage. But then the, the flights stopped coming from, from Munich. And this was also reason that this-- the third fellow couldn't get out, the Lothar Levine. And we didn't have any [LAUGHS] income anymore, you know? So it was all black market there in, in Spain. You could only-- they only had fish. They had fish, yes. They had fish, which they sold. They'd fry it on the street. And you could buy fish.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you could buy bread. But the bread was very hard. It was stale. And they sold it in the-- in the red-light district. They'd, they'd pull it out of their panties if you asked for bread. And they sold it to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he bring the horseradish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He brought a horseradish. Yes, he brought a horseradish. He must have had-- I don't know. He must have had 20 or 30 of these roots, which we sold in the black market. Probably they don't have sandy-- because horseradish grows in, in, in, in, in sandy ground, you know? And we sold also the horseradish in the black market. And we exchanged it sometimes for, for bread or for fish. And that's how we lived. And we started to sing on the street. Herbert, he had the harmonica, you know. And the German songs were very much admired at the time in Spain, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was Franco's Spain, you know? And there were still-- many German officers were in Spain. Germany had a great influence in Spain. I mean, on account of Germany they won the war-- their, their civil war. And the-- so Herbert, he had a little harmonica, a Hohner harmonica. And he knew how to play the harmonica. And he played, and I sang on the Rambla de Cataluna. We passed a hat. We didn't get too much, I tell you, huh? Because he played better than I sang, that I must say. And that's how you survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's how we survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in Barcelona? [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, let me tell you about-- we always checked with that-- with that office there. I mean, we, we went there every day or every second day, you know, just in case something should come up. And one day, that manager, who was nice, he said, you know, there is a-- you know, there is a Jewish organization here. Why don't you check in with that organization? Now, we didn't know about this, because, of course, that organization, they never waited for you at the airport because no one was coming in by plane. They were all coming in by train. They were coming through France. It was a sealed train. That train was sealed. The Jews-- those Jews who could get out, who had the visas, they went through France and-- in a sealed train. They went through the Pyrenees. Irun was the, the border city. And they came to, to, to Madrid or to Barcelona on the train. So they were accustomed to pick up the people from the train station, you know. And we never knew about this.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said, why don't you go there? We went there. And they were very nice. It was a, a, a little branch office, which was supported by the Joint Distribution Committee.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they said, all right now. Forget about what you're doing here, what, what you did for the Lufthansa. You don't have to sing anymore. And you don't have to play the harmonica anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: You can play-- you can now help us. You can also carry suitcases, but now for us. And we pay for your board, and we pay for your-- for your lodgings, you know? And then we were in a rooming house with a board. We were four or five sleeping in one room. But it was like a palace to us. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we-- then they also said, what kind of papers do you have? They asked us.  And we showed them the visa to Liberia, the ship. And we told them about the Reiseburo Rominger, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they said, listen, you won't be able to do this. And they got an extension for us, for the-- for the-- for the two weeks we had on that transit visa in Spain. And they sent a-- we had a waiting number, both of us. My number was 20,687. And it wouldn't have been up for another six months or so. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: A waiting number for--

speaker: interviewee
text: For-- to go to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: The quota. Again, it's the quota. So they, they said that they will try. They sent a, a, a cable every day-- long cables to the United States, the Joint Distribution Committee. And they told them about our case, that we were here in, in Barcelona, that we had a visa to Liberia, which we couldn't land, et cetera, et cetera. They knew-- they knew the whole-- everything. And whether they couldn't try to get us a, a visa to go to the United States. We had our numbers. They gave us our waiting numbers, et cetera, et cetera. We were registered. And to make a long story short, so we got a visa to the-- for the United States. And friends of mine, they asked me whether I could get some money and that I-- for the-- for the passage to, to go to, to the States. And I had a, a, a cousin of my mother, who had given me the affidavit when I was still in Stuttgart. But that affidavit was no good because my waiting number was not up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had the affidavit, but I couldn't use it. But I had the affidavit with me. And I showed it to them. So they got a new affidavit. And they got me the, the visa to go to the-- to the-- to the United States. And your mother's cousin was in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was my mother's cousin once removed. He had come to the United States in the '20s.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he had a business in the United States. He was doing quite well. He gave me the affidavit and he paid half of the, the, the passage with the boat. And the other half was paid by two friends of mine who had immigrated earlier, whom I also sent the telegrams through the Joint Distribution Committee. And they both paid about $120 each. That cousin of my mother paid about $240. It was about $480 or $485. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I left from Barcelona.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you leave? Do you remember that date?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I left Spain or Germany?

speaker: interviewer
text: Both, actually.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I left-- I left Munich at the, the second part of July, '41. And I left Barcelona towards the end of August in 1941. It must have been very close to the last day of August. And Herbert, he got his passage from, from Bilbao. That's at the west coast of Spain. Barcelona's at the east coast of Spain. So he left from Bilbao. I left from, from Barcelona. And the ship I was on was We were about 580 people on that ship. It was a very small ship. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: We were about 300 people in the hall of the ship, the aisle part of the ship, you know? In fact, there was-- in the-- in the-- in the center of the ship, there was about this high-- there was always water in there about this high. I don't know why. It was all rusty in there. And we had bunks, you know, wooden bunks three high.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we lived on that ship. They-- I must say, we didn't-- they fed us. They fed us with watermelons and, and white bread. That's, that's what we got. Of course, I got seasick again. I couldn't eat-- even eat the white bread and the watermelons. I felt terrible. And as the Inspector Koch had mentioned, we were stopped by the English in-- at Gibraltar. They came. They boarded our ship. They took a look around. They wanted to-- they wanted to see papers of some of these immigrants. And then after about three or four hours, they let us pass. They inspected the ship. And then from there, we went to, to Tangiers, to Morocco, where we also stayed for about three or four hours. We couldn't get off the ship, of course. From there, we went to the Canary Islands, to Tenerife. And as we drove into that little port in Tenerife, there was a German submarine right next to us. [LAUGHS] Yes. Well, we finally arrived in, in New York. I think it took us about two weeks. I think it took us about two weeks. We arrived in New York. And I was one of three or four who couldn't get off the boat because my papers or the affidavit was not 100% correct. And they took me to Ellis Island. And I was a few weeks at Ellis Island. I became the ping-pong champion in Ellis Island. And I enjoyed it very, very much. I figured if this is a prison, my goodness, I would like to know what the outside looks like because the food was excellent. I mean, we got a tray. But there was food I never seen in my life. There were doughnuts. There was chocolate milk. I'd never seen anything like it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your first memories of being outside of Europe were positive.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, were positive outside of that first night [LAUGHS] on the boat. Before we, we, we-- when we arrived in-- I think it was in Brooklyn. I'm not even sure about that. I think it was at a Brooklyn Pier. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We, we stayed there during the night. And the next day, they took us to Ellis Island, the three or four of us. And there was a Spanish sailor, a, a fairy. And he was after me. And I don't want to go into details, but I, I got out of it as a virgin, [LAUGHS] if I may say so. But he gave me, before I got to Ellis Island-- because he had-- he had gotten out. I mean, they were allowed to go off, off, off the ship. And he had brought already all kinds of goodies, you know, which he offered to me. And I ate them. I didn't-- I didn't think about his, his bad intentions, which came afterwards. But I got away with it. And he was nice. He was not too aggressive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the date that you arrived in the US?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I, I would have to look it up, but it was in September.

speaker: interviewer
text: September.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in September of 1941.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And I realized the United States went into war in, in December, December, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] December of 1941--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --against Japan. Pearl Harbor was attacked December 6, 1941.
